coredata.dc:title: Indole-imidazole hybrid Schiff base for the selective detection of the explosive picric acid via fluorescence “turn-off” process: Experimental and theoretical DFT-D3 study
coredata.dc:creator: Hemalatha, V.
Vijayakumar, V.
coredata.dc:description: A compound containing a heteroatom and Schiff base, 3-(4,5-Diphenyl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-1H-indole (INBIL) has been synthesized through the one-pot condensation of indole-3-carbaldehyde with benzil in ethanol and characterized using various analytical parameters like FT-IR, NMR; its thermal and chemical stability also established. The synthesized INBIL showed a 'turn off' luminescent property that has been used to detect picric acid. This luminescent sensor has a broad emission band at 415 nm in acetonitrile due to the conjugation of its double bond. It is worth noting that INBIL can be recycled through TEA treatment. The chemosensor exhibited the highest sensitivity with Ksv = 1.51 × 10−6 M, and a detection limit of 2.91 × 10−7 M, with perfect linearity up to 22 µL. The binding stoichiometry ratio between INBIL and picric acid was predicted to be 1:1 using Job's plot, which was confirmed by 1H NMR analysis. Finally, DFT-D3 calculations were used to calculate the excited molecule's singlet and triplet energy states.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Indole imidazole hybrid Schiff base
Turn-off sensor
ICT-PET-FRET
Picric acid
TEA treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of receptor INBIL
Selectivity and interference by absorption studies
Selectivity and interference by emission studies
Evaluation of association constant Ka and B-H equation and time-resolved fluorescence studies
Reproducibility and analytical applications of INBIL
Detection of PA in the matchstick powder sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: VV and VH gratefully acknowledge thanking the VIT management for characterization support and infrastructure. Authors thank full VIT management for seed grant (Grant number SG20230086 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TGA spectra of compound INBIL.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum and (b) Fluorescence emission spectrum of the INBIL (Ex = 360 nm) in acetonitrile solvent system.

(a) Selectivity spectra of INBIL (2 × 10−5) (40 µL) with other interfering species (40 µL) (b) Bar graph representation of INBIL with other interfering species (c) Interference spectra of INBIL (2 × 10−5) (40 µL) with other interfering species in the presence of PA (40 µL) (d) Bar graph representation of INBIL with other interfering species in the presence of PA (40 µL).
(a) Sensitivity experiment of INBIL with difference concentration (4 µL to 96 µL) of PA. (b) Job′s plot of INBIL with PA.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of INBIL in pure acetonitrile with the addition of 22 µl of PA (λex = 360 nm, λem = 415 nm) (b) Overlapping of the absorption band of INBIL+PA with the emission profile of the INBIL.

(a) Emission spectra (λex=360 nm) of the probe INBIL in the presence of various interfering species, b) Bar diagram of fluorescence emission at 415 nm of INBIL upon addition of various interfering species in acetonitrile, c) Cross-interference of PA with various interfering species in acetonitrile medium (at 415 nm, λex = 360 nm). d) Bar diagram of cross-interference of PA with various interfering species in acetonitrile medium (at 415 nm, λex = 360 nm).

a) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and the PA concentration b) The Linearity plot derived from fluorescence titrations of INBIL with PA, and c) Stern–Volmer plot for complex INBIL upon incremental addition of PA in CH3CN (inset) at lower concentration of PA, emission quenching linearity relationship.
INBIL′s fluorescence lifetime decay profile with various concentrations of PA in CH3CN.
1H NMR spectrum of INBIL and INBIL+PA in DMSO‑d6 at 25 °C.
HH COSY enlarged spectra of INBIL and INBIL+PA in DMSO‑d6 at 25 °C.

(a) HOMO and LUMO energies of the INBIL Fluorophore unit representative and nitro explosives obtained from the DFT study. (b) Sensing mechanism toward PA detection by an INBIL Fluorophore unit. (c) The DFT computed molecular electrostatic potential map of INBIL and PA.
a) Optimized structures for probe INBIL and addition of INBIL+PA. b) Frontier molecular orbitals and HOMO-LUMO energy level diagrams of INBIL and INBIL+PA.
a) Reproducibility spectra of INBIL (2 × 10−5) with PA and TEA, b) Bar graph representation of INBIL reversibility.
The reversibility studies under the daylight and portable UV lamp (365 nm).
Visual color change under UV light upon gradual addition of 10−3 M PA in ACN to 10−5 M solution of INBIL in ACN solution (I) 0 μl of PA, (II) 20 μl of PA.
TLC under a portable UV lamp (365 nm) changes INBIL and INBIL+PA.
Studies of INBIL towards MSPS species in acetonitrile medium (λex=360 nm).
Absorbance spectra of INBIL in the presence of 20 µl of PA in acetonitrile media at different time scales. The spectra are almost the same for about 60 min.
Synthesis of Receptor INBIL.
Plausible reaction binding mechanism of INBIL with PA.
Summary of proton chemical shifts (a) and carbon chemical shifts (b) of INBIL.
Structure of the quencher used in the present study.
Absorption and emission data for INBIL.
The comparative study of the present work with the previously reported works.

head.ce:title: Indole-imidazole hybrid Schiff base for the selective detection of the explosive picric acid via fluorescence “turn-off” process: Experimental and theoretical DFT-D3 study

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore-632014, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Advanced Sciences, Vellore Institute of Technology, Vellore-632014, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A compound containing a heteroatom and Schiff base, 3-(4,5-Diphenyl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)-1H-indole (INBIL) has been synthesized through the one-pot condensation of indole-3-carbaldehyde with benzil in ethanol and characterized using various analytical parameters like FT-IR, NMR; its thermal and chemical stability also established. The synthesized INBIL showed a 'turn off' luminescent property that has been used to detect picric acid. This luminescent sensor has a broad emission band at 415 nm in acetonitrile due to the conjugation of its double bond. It is worth noting that INBIL can be recycled through TEA treatment. The chemosensor exhibited the highest sensitivity with Ksv = 1.51 × 10−6 M, and a detection limit of 2.91 × 10−7 M, with perfect linearity up to 22 µL. The binding stoichiometry ratio between INBIL and picric acid was predicted to be 1:1 using Job's plot, which was confirmed by 1H NMR analysis. Finally, DFT-D3 calculations were used to calculate the excited molecule's singlet and triplet energy states.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Supplementary file has been submitted along with the mansucript

ce:section.ce:para: There have been numerous small organic luminescent materials developed over the last decade that can detect explosive nitro-phenolic compounds. These compounds, which are designated as reputed toxic pollutants, are dangerous for the environment and for mankind in nature [1,2]. Examples of these compounds include picric acid (PA), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, C6H3N3O7), nitrobenzene, 1,3-dinitrobenzene, 2,4-dinitrotoluene, and 2,6-dinitrotoluene. PA is particularly explosive and is even more so than trinitrotoluene (TNT). It is the most well-known nitro-aromatic secondary explosive, according to the Occupational Safety and Health Administration [3–5]. PA can cause serious health effects, such as skin, eye irritation, liver damage, gastritis, aplastic anemia, and cyanosis. It can damage the organs involved in the respiratory system and pose a significant environmental threat [6,7]. It is commonly used in the pharmaceutical, chemical, and dye industries, as well as in matchsticks and photographic emulsions. PA has a higher explosive violence than TNT and 2,4-DNT [8,9]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop selective and sensitive PA detection sensors in light of these facts [10,11].

The detection of PA through fluorescence methods has not been extensively explored, as it has mainly been accomplished through several other methods. These include latent fingerprint visualization, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), ion-mobility spectrometry (IMS), flame atomic absorption spectrometry, gas chromatography (GC), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), electrochemistry, colorimetric, X-ray diffraction, fluorometric, and mass spectrometry [12,13]. However, all of these methods have a common drawback - requiring complex instrumentation that is both expensive to install and requires experienced labour. Conversely, fluorescence studies offer high selectivity, straightforward instrumentation, quick reactions, and critically, minimal expenses. This makes fluorescence studies an undoubtedly superior choice.

The detection of PA is of great concern due to its presence as a major contaminant in groundwater [14,15] and its permitted concentration in groundwater is 0.001 mg L − 1 according to WHO. While various materials have been utilized for detecting PA such as organic gels, small molecular dyes, polymers, and metal-organic frameworks [16–19], fluorescence quenching-based sensing has proven to be a simpler and more sensitive method. Despite the development of fluorescence-based sensors, their low binding affinity, limited selectivity, and poor detection limits have hampered their practical application. On the other hand, electron-rich conjugated sensors have been found to be ideal candidates for detecting nitro-phenolic aromatic explosives [20,21], making them a promising solution for detecting PA.

Chemosensors are organic molecules that can react with target analytes, and they rely on various components like hydrazine, urea, thiourea, imidazole, pyrrole, amide, phenols, indoles, oximes, hydrazones, pyridine, phenazine, imine, Schiff base, and so on. A sensor needs to be affordable, easy to produce, and able to respond quickly through colour or fluorescence for use in the market [22]. However, sometimes sensor molecules may exhibit a delayed response due to a lack of conjugation and other deformities. Schiff bases are commonly used as chemosensors to detect various metal ions due to their simple reaction mechanism, easy accessibility to binding acceptors, and low cost of reactants [23,24]. Luminescent chemosensors have also gained significant interest due to their high sensitivity, quick response, selectivity, and ease of handling, making them widely applicable in medical, environmental, and forensic research fields. Schiff base organic compound (SOC) is a popular choice for low-cost, easy-to-make chemosensors, as it can be made using inexpensive organic precursors [25].

It's fascinating to see the various potential uses of imidazole moieties, ranging from anion and biomolecule sensors to fluorescent protein tags and more. It's also interesting to note that although some recent imidazole-based fluorophore sensors have been explored for detecting PA and other nitro-phenolic aromatic explosives [26–28], there seems to be a lack of research in this area. It would be exciting to see more studies conducted to explore the potential applications of imidazole moieties in detecting such explosives.

In the present communication through extensive theoretical analysis, we have determined that INBIL exhibits the most selective turn-off response to PA when compared to other explosives. This is significant from a homeland security perspective because the prototype can detect PA in both solid and solution states, making it a valuable tool for identifying this hazardous substance.

We have successfully synthesized a luminophore that contains electron-donating indole and an electron-deficient imidazole group which is effective in detecting trace amounts of PA due to their strong binding with the acidic PA. This binding leads to fluorescence quenching and shows chemo-sensing behaviour. The presence of nitrogen in the luminophore makes it more basic. The characterization of the luminophore molecule INBIL was established through 1H, 13C NMR, and mass spectrometry measurements. The electron-rich fluorescent quenching can also interact with the electron-deficient imidazole moiety through photo-induced electron transfer. Fortunately, this luminophore has high selectivity and good sensitivity for detecting PA in the solution, even among different interfering explosives. In conclusion, our imidazole luminophore shows potential for detecting nitro-phenolic aromatics.

The experiment utilized chemicals and solvents of analytical and spectroscopic quality sourced from commercial chemical suppliers. The solvent acetonitrile (CH3CN) was purchased from AVRA Chemicals. The experiment was conducted in an acetonitrile medium, with various nitro and phenolic derivatives including 2,6-di‑tert‑butyl‑4-methyl phenol (BHT), resorcinol (Res), chloramphenicol (CAP), benzophenone (BP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) and 2-amino-4-nitrophenol (ANP). The samples were characterized using a variety of analytical techniques, including 1H NMR, 13C NMR, UV–Vis absorption spectral studies, Fourier transform infrared spectroscope IR Affinity-1 Shimadzu FT-IR spectrometer, and thermogravimetric analyses (TGA). The samples were also analysed using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer and a Shimadzu LC-MS 2020 spectrometer with an LC10ADVP binary pump, and electrospray ionization mass spectral analysis (ESI-MS). The functional group of the sample was characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectroscope IR Affinity-1 Shimadzu FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. KBr (IR grade) was used to prepare the samples as pellets. The TGA was carried out at ambient – 1200 °C on the SDT Q600–Horizontal Balance & Furnace at nitrogen inert gas atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of receptor INBIL
ce:section.ce:para:
INBIL was synthesized using the Debus-Radziszewski method. A mixture of indol-3-carboxaldehyde (1) (1.3777 mmol; 0.2 g), benzil (2) (1.3777 mmol; 0.28 g) in ethanol (6 mL) was taken and a catalytic amount of glacial acetic acid and ammonium acetate (3) (4.1333 mmol; 0.318 g) was added. The mixture was stirred at 78 °C for 2 h. TLC on silica gel-coated plates was used to monitor the reaction, which was visualized with UV light. After completion, the solid product was washed with hexane, filtered, dried, and obtained as an off-white product with a yield of 84 %. The melting point of the product was found to be 285 °C (C23H17N3). The synthetic route of INBIL is clearly shown in Scheme 1
[29]. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d6, ppm, TMS): δ 12.27 (s, 1H, imidazole NH), 11.36 (s, 1H, indole NH), 8.48 (d, J = 7.2 Hz, 1H, at C4 of indole moiety), 8.00 (d, J = 2.8 Hz, 1H, at C2 of indole moiety); (this signal found to be in correlation with the signal at 11.36 ppm and hence the –NH proton of the indol moiety assigned to 11.36 ppm), 7.64 (d, J = 7.6 Hz, 2H, ortho protons of phenyl ring), 7.53 (d, J = 7.2 Hz, 2H, ortho protons of phenyl ring), 7.47–7.43 (m, 3H, meta and para protons of the phenyl ring), 7.36 (t, J = 7.6 Hz, 1H, para proton of the phenyl ring, partially merged with the another triplet at 7.33), 7.33 (t, J = 7.6 Hz, 2H, meta protons of the phenyl ring, partially merged with the another triplet at 7.36), 7.28 (d, J = 7.2 Hz, 1H, at C7 of indole moiety), 7.18 (t, J = 7.2 Hz, 1H, at C6 of indole moiety), 7.14 (t, J = 7.2 Hz, 1H, at C5 of indole moiety) Chemical shift values for the protons of the molecule INBIL has been assigned unambiguously using H, HCOSY. The NOESY spectrum of the molecule revealed the proximity of the indole CH with the two –NH functions of the molecule, and this could be possible only if both indole and imidazole moieties are orthogonal to each other. 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d6, ppm, TMS): 144.2, 136.7, 136.4, 136.2, 132.1, 129.1, 128.8, 128.7 128.6, 128.0, 127.4, 126.6, 126.2, 124.2, 122.3, 122.0, 120.2, 112.1, 107.3 (Fig.S3). FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3416, 1604, 1494, 1447 (Fig.S7). The careful examination of the HSQC spectrum reveals that the proton signals at 8.48 and 8.00 ppm correlate with the carbon signals at 122.0 and 124.2 ppm respectively and hence it has been assigned to the C-4 and C-2 carbons of indole moiety respectively. The proton signals at 7.28 and 7.18 ppm were found to correlate with the carbon signal at 126.6 and 122.3 ppm respectively and hence it has been assigned to the C-7 and C-6 carbons of indole moiety respectively. The proton signal is 7.14 ppm in correlation with the carbon signal 120.2 ppm and is assigned to the C-5 carbon of the indole moiety. The ortho protons of both the aryl rings appear at 7.64 and 7.53 in correlation with the 127.4 and 128.6 ppm and hence are assigned to the ortho protons of both the aryl rings. Among both the ortho proton signals the relatively upfield proton signal was found to be in correlation with the relatively downfield carbon signal. The proton signals at 7.36 (para) and 7.33 (meta) ppm are in correlation with the carbon signals at 128.0 and 128.7 ppm respectively and assigned to the para and meta carbons of the aryl ring. The multiplet in the range of 7.47–7.43 ppm was found to be in correlation with the carbon signals at 128.8 and 112.1 ppm respectively and is assigned to another para and meta carbons of the other ring. All the proton-bearing carbons have been assigned unambiguously using the HSQC spectrum and the remaining eight non-proton bearing carbon signals are assigned tentatively and are included in Chart 1 . The proton and carbon chemical shift assignments of compound INBIL have been summarized and are given as Chart 1. CHN Anal. Calcd: C, 82.36; H, 5.11; N, 12.53; Found: C, 81.98; H, 5.90; N, 11.98. IR (ν) cm−1: 3404 (-NH stretching frequency); 1621 (C = N), 1598 (-C = C bending frequency) 729, 696 (aromatic CH bending) cm−1. HR-Mass; Calculated mass of INBIL: 335.1422, Obtained mass of INBIL: 336.1507 (Fig.S11). LC-Mass: Calculated mass of INBI+PA: 564.1393, Obtained mass of INBIL+PA: 565.9000 (Fig.S12). UV–Vis λmax/nm (ε/dm3 mol−1 cm−1) (CH3CN) INBIL: 306 (Fig. 2a), INBIL+PA: 374, FL emission λmax/nm (CH3CN) INBIL: 415 (Fig. 2b). Chart 2

The working solution of INBIL were made with concentrations of 2 × 10−5 M, respectively. Other substances that could interfere with the experiment, such as BHT, Res, CAP, BP, 4-NP, 3-NP, 2-NP, ANP, and PA, were also prepared at a concentration of 1 × 10−3 M. During the titration experiments using the essential stock solution and working solution of INBIL along with the interfering substances, no complex formation or color change was observed. This indicates that INBIL is selective towards nitroaromatic compounds and does not bind with interfering substances. The concentration of INBIL in the sensing study was estimated to be 5 mg/mL through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). These findings suggest that INBIL can be a reliable and selective sensor for nitroaromatic compounds.

Based on the TGA curve, it can be observed that there is a single-stage decomposition process of INBIL, which results in a significant weight loss. This process does not form any intermediate products and transforms the entire sample. The decomposition process involves three specific thermal processes: evaporation (resulting in the loss of volatile components and weight), decomposition (leading to a continuous decrease in weight over a specific temperature range), and combustion (combustible components undergo combustion, resulting in a weight loss).

The TGA technique was used to analyze the INBIL compound in a nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 20°C min−1 to determine its thermal parameters. The obtained data (as shown in Fig. 1 ) indicates that INBIL has good thermal stability. The decomposition temperature starts at around 150–160°C (2 % weight loss), which is due to the loss of organic solvent. The compound starts to break down at around 305°C (97 % weight loss) and gradually decomposes up to 500°C. This essential characteristic is crucial in maintaining the structure and morphology of the compound, particularly in optical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference by absorption studies
ce:section.ce:para: We hypothesized that INBIL could serve as a reliable sensor for detecting nitro-phenolic aromatics, particularly picric acid. This is because the compound's tripodal arrangement of N-sites enables it to accept H-bonds, which makes it an excellent choice for selective recognition. Fluorescence spectroscopy showed that the probe INBIL displayed an absorption band at 306 nm (Fig. 2 a) and emission at 415 nm (Fig. 2b).

It has been discovered that the compound INBIL does not absorb light between the wavelengths of 368 and 500 nm. The addition of other nitro and phenolic aromatic derivatives, such as BHT, Res, CAP, BP, 4-NP, 3-NP, 2-NP, and ANP (all at 1 × 10−3 M concentration), to INBIL did not show any new absorption band at 374 nm (as shown in Fig. 3 a). However, when a PA (1 × 10−3 M) solution was gradually (4 µL increment) added to the INBIL receptor, a new peak at approximately 374 nm was observed, in addition to the characteristic absorption peak of INBIL at around 306 nm (as shown in Fig. 4 a). The quenching spectrum in the case of PA showed a redshift of approximately 68 nm, which indicates easy intramolecular charge transfer. A competitive analyte test or cross-interference studies (using a 2 × 10−5 solution) concluded that other analytes present at an equivalent amount could not affect the detection of PA (as shown in Fig. 3a). This means that even when other interfering species are present, INBIL can selectively detect PA (as shown in Fig. 3c). Therefore, it is possible to use the ligand INBIL to detect PA, even when it is present along with other interfering species.

In the MEP diagram (Fig. 11c), we can observe a high accumulation of electrons in the indole and imidazole NH atoms (shown in blue), with the electrons resonating towards the tripodal nitrogen (shown in red). The electrophile PA accepts a lone pair of electrons, forming a hydrogen bond with the tripodal nitrogen. This resonating behaviour has been confirmed by 1H NMR titration, which shows that the imidazole and indole proton are shifted downfield. Scheme 2 represents the resonance behaviour of INBIL. INBIL contains electron-donating groups (EDG) at the N1 position, which makes the nitrogen lone pair available for chemo sensing and influences the photo physical properties.

The INBIL molecule absorbs light at a wavelength of approximately 306 nm due to a type of electron transition called π-π* transition. When PA is added to the solution, the absorbance band shifts to around 374 nm, indicating a different type of electron transition called n-π* transition. The formed INBIL+PA, absorbs light at higher wavelengths due to the presence of electron-withdrawing nitro groups. The colour change and quenching of the fluorescence is a result of electronic interactions between the tripodal nitrogen and PA hydroxyl hydrogen, as well as the PET (Photoinduced Electron Transfer) process. According to Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) where the excitation energy transfer from the fluorophore to PA and Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) process, the excited state electrons are excited from the fluorophore to PA. This is the main reason behind the colour change of INBIL. Based on the experiment conducted, it was determined that the INBIL with PA stoichiometric ratio is 1:1. The absorption and emission were recorded by altering the volume of INBIL and PA, while keeping the total volume of both solutions constant and their concentrations unchanged. The absorption data obtained were plotted and presented in Fig. 4b, which clearly shows the 1:1 binding stoichiometric phenomenon between guest PA and INBIL at a 0.5-mole fraction. This was further confirmed by mass spectral analysis as shown in Fig. S9.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference by emission studies
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of electron-donating functional group amine in INBIL has been confirmed by FT-IR, indicating its potential for detecting electron-deficient nitro-phenolic aromatic compounds like PA, and their fluorescence behaviour. The photophysical properties of INBIL have been studied in acetonitrile, and the absorption and emission maximum have been summarized in Table 1 . To understand the interactions between INBIL and PA in the excited states, fluorescence titration measurements were carried out. A decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity was observed during the gradual addition of PA1 × 10−3 M (up to 22 µL), into the probe as depicted in Fig. 5 a. The strong spectral overlap of the emission and absorbance spectra of the donor (INBIL) and acceptor (PA) suggest that the fluorescence quenching may be due to the FRET process in Fig. 5b.

It was discovered that the efficiency of a sensor could be assessed by evaluating the inherent emission profile of INBIL in the presence of PA. The results confirmed that INBIL has a remarkable sensitivity and selectivity towards PA, which is depicted in Fig. 6 a and the bar graph in Fig. 6b. When luminophore in dilute solutions were treated with PA, the solution changed from colourless to yellow upon exposure to UV light, as demonstrated in Fig. 6c. The fluorescence responses showed no interference from external analytes. Table 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of association constant Ka and B-H equation and time-resolved fluorescence studies
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted an analysis of titrations of the Schiff base INBIL with the PA. Based on this analysis, we conducted linear curve fitting to determine the regression constant R2, association constant Ka, and LOD (Limit of Detection). We found the LOD value to be 2.91 × 10−7 M, which we calculated using the well-established equation LOD=3σ/k. Here, σ represents the variation from the standard value σ=0.02046 (1-R2), and k is the slope of the curve Fig. 7 b.

In order to understand the quenching mechanism studied by Stern-Volmer F0/F = 1+Ksv[Q], it is important to note that F0 represents the initial fluorescence intensity, while F represents the emission intensity in the presence of PA. After performing the Stern-Volmer (SV) analysis, it was observed that a linear pattern emerged at lower concentrations of the analyte PA. However, at higher concentrations of PA, the plot between intensity changes and PA concentration resulted in a bending curve that arced upwards. This indicated the presence of both static and dynamic quenching (as shown in Fig. 7d). The quenching constant (Ksv), calculated to be 1.51 × 10−6 M, confirms the INBIL method as a viable way to detect PA.

To understand how the quenching mechanism works, the absorption spectrum of INBIL+PA and the emission spectrum of INBIL were combined with the excitation and emission spectra of the sensor. The resulting merged spectra clearly showed a significant overlap between INBIL+PA and the sensor's emission spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5b. This suggests that the fluorescence quenching mechanism in a large area may be due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), as reported in [30,31].

To investigate how INBIL is quenched, we measured the average fluorescence lifetimes of INBIL in the presence and absence of PA. We utilized a time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) setup to obtain the results shown in Fig. 6. The values of fluorescence lifetimes are given in Table S1. There are two broad categories of fluorescence quenching mechanisms - static and dynamic quenching [32]. In both quenching processes, the fluorophore and quencher interact at the molecular level. In static quenching, they form a non-emissive ground-state complex, and the fluorescent lifetime does not change. In dynamic quenching, however, an excited-state process occurs when the fluorophore and quencher collide. In this case, a photo-induced electron transfer occurs from the fluorophore to the quencher, thereby decreasing the average lifetime of INBIL [33]. The Stern-Volmer plot for PA showed a mutual static and dynamic quenching effect. The decay process of INBIL gradually increased with lifetime, and it is evident from Table S1 that fluorescence lifetime values increase upon adding PA in the concentration range of 0.2 μl-2.8 μl. Furthermore, Fig. 7d shows that a linear behaviour is observed in the time-resolved S-V plot, suggesting that fluorescence quenching is due to the dynamic quenching process. Fig. 8

The acidic nature of PA enables the use of 1H NMR titration as a reliable method for its detection. The acidic protons swiftly transfer to the basic functional group in the luminophores, resulting in fluorescence quenching and a robust acidic PA interaction. The interaction between the luminophores and PA was further comprehended through the 1H NMR technique. As per Fig. 9 , the addition of picric acid to INBIL leads to the imidazole N-atom attacking the oxygen of the hydroxyl function (a), causing the delocalization of the double bond (and the resonance Scheme 2). This process induces a positive charge on both -NH groups, causing their shift to the downfield. The signal at δ 12.27 (s, 1H, imidazole NH) shifted to δ14.40 ppm; the signal at δ 11.36 (s, 1H, indole NH) shifted to 12.30 ppm. The H, HCOSY spectra of INBIL and INBIL+PA ( Fig. 10 ) revealed the same. Further, the close examination of Figs. 9 and 10 revealed that the signal at δ 8.48 (d, J = 7.2 Hz, 1H, at C4 of indole moiety of INBIL-designated as signal “d” shifted upfield to δ 8.15 ppm (in INBIL+PA) since it gains the electron density after the addition of PA. On the other hand, the signal of the proton at C2 of indole moiety appeared at δ 8.00 ppm (d, J = 2.8 Hz, 1H, at C2 of indole moiety- designated as signal “c” is shifted downfield at δ 8.30 ppm after the addition of PA. The density functional theory studies also support all these observations and suggest a strong electrostatic interaction between PA and INBIL. These NMR studies confirm the interaction of imidazole nitrogen with PA and demonstrate that the PET process could play a major role in the fluorescence sensing of the INBIL luminophores towards PA.

Based on the experimental findings from the UV–Vis, FT-IR, and 1H NMR spectroscopic studies, the mechanistic pathway is presented in Scheme 2, which also agrees with the theoretical findings. The delocalization of the double bond may cause a significant increase in conjugation in the INBIL that favours charge transfer, hence the spectral change/shift was observed. The compound INBIL is emissive (cyan colour under UV) due to π - π stacking interactions, which permit the flow of electrons. When picric acid is added to the INBIL, it intercalates between these π electrons through the H-bonding interaction. This causes the fluorescence to be quenched as the picric acid molecule serves as an electron acceptor. The electrons are then drawn towards the picric acid, leading to the quenching of the fluorescence.

The fluorescent quenching process is affected by various factors including structural collapse and surface interaction, which involves FRET and PET. To understand the quenching mechanism that occurs when PA is added to INBIL, a density functional theory (DFT) calculation was performed. The calculation utilized a B3LYP/6–31 G (d,p) basis set and Gaussian 09 software [41,20]. Table S2 presents the HOMO and LUMO energies. Notably, PA has the lowest energy LUMO (−4.49 eV) among the analytes in Fig. 11 a. This indicates that PA is more likely to accept electrons from an excited fluorophore unit of the INBIL than the other analytes.

The optimized geometries of INBIL and INBIL+PA, as well as the Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO), are shown in Fig. 12 a. and b. respectively. The HOMO electron density of INBIL is evenly distributed between the indole 3-carboxaldehyde and benzil moieties, whereas the LUMO electrons are mainly localized on the electron-withdrawing centre. When PA is added, the electron density in the HOMO of INBIL+PA spreads more on the INBIL moiety, while its LUMO lies on the PA side, owing to the -NO2 electron-withdrawing group. The FMO investigation confirms the PET process taking place after adding PA to INBIL, which is indicated by the lowering of the interaction energy. The energy gap of INBIL decreases significantly from 4.01166 eV to 1.85949 eV after adding PA. Therefore, the study concludes that INBIL and PA exhibit favourable complexation, indicating that ICT flows preferentially from the free sensor compound to PA. Figs. 13 and 14

To better understand the electronic structure at the molecular level of the compound, we have calculated the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) as presented in Fig. 11 (c). The MEP colours indicate that the red colour represents a negative electrostatic potential for nucleophilic attack reactions, while the blue colour represents a positive electrostatic potential for electrophilic attack reactions. The green colour represents a neutral electrostatic potential. and the green color represents the neutral electrostatic potential. Following resonance, the tripodal nitrogen donates a pair of electrons to the electron-deficient PA. The electron density values of the title compound are at −6.477 × 10−3 on the negative side and 6.477 × 10−3 on the positive side. The red color on the MEP typically indicates high electron deficiency, while the blue color indicates less electron deficiency. Fig. 11 (c) demonstrates the calculated MEP at the B3LYP/6–31 G (d, p) level of theory (Iso value: 0.0004). The interaction between INBIL and PA is plausible as INBIL has electron-rich groups and may cause quenching. When the analyte and fluorophore have the interaction, it causes spectral shifts or the appearance of new peaks, altering the fluorophore's absorption spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and analytical applications of INBIL
ce:section.ce:para: As part of our experiment, we performed a reversibility test to determine whether the imidazole derivative could be reversed in the presence of PA [42,43]. We added triethylamine (TEA) to the INBIL+PA solution, which resulted in the restoration of fluorescent intensity. We repeated the ON/OFF fluorescent behaviour by adding PA to the INBIL, indicating that it can once again function as a PA sensor. These findings indicate that the INBIL sensor has good reproducibility.

Many sensors are known to exhibit a visual color change along with the change in fluorescence signalling when subjected to the presence of PA in the sensor solution. Therefore, it is crucial to have a solution-mode visual detection of PA for real-time applications. For visual detection, a 10−3 M PA was slowly added to 10−5 M solutions of INBIL, (Fig. 15 ). As expected, the fluorescence changed from blue to non-fluorescent upon the addition of PA, which is consistent with the previous findings.

By considering the factors of portability and cost efficiency, test strips are made from commercially available TLC sheets. The drop casting method has been performed on the fluorescence of the INBIL with the addition of PA solution (10−3 M). To sense in contact mode, TLC sheets were cut into 3 cm2 pieces dipped into the INBIL sensor solution, and then dried under reduced pressure. After that, a fresh test strip was used, and 10 µl of PA solution (10−3 M) was drop-casted on it. The strips were observed under UV light, and dark black spots were visible for PA (Fig. 16 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA in the matchstick powder sample
ce:section.ce:para: A 500 mg matchstick head powder sample (MSPS) was collected and ground well by using a pestle and mortar. 20 ml of acetonitrile solution is added to this powder and sonicated for 15 min. The dispersed solution is filtered and carried out for analysis [44]. Filtered MSPS solution is added to probe INBIL and shaken well. The fluorescence intensity of INBIL is decreased suddenly as represented in Fig. 17 .

It is important for practical applications that a sensor is not only selective for the target compound, but also photo stable for the required sensing time. PA is known for its instantaneous absorbance response, making it a good candidate for rapid detection. Organic probes are generally not photo stable and their absorbance intensity changes over time. In this context, the photo stability of the system is a beneficial factor for the application. The absorbance intensity of INBIL in the presence of PA does not change with time scale as demonstrated in Fig. 18 .

A fluorescent sensor called INBIL has been developed to detect PA, a harmful chemical, even at deficient concentrations (2.91 × 10−7 M). This sensor works by "turning off" its fluorescence when exposed to PA, and this process has been studied using emission spectroscopy. INBIL can interact with PA in a 1:1 ratio and it can be easily reused. The detection limit for INBIL is impressively low, thanks to the PET effect, which allows electrons to transfer easily from the luminophore to the PA. The HOMO-LUMO energy levels and emission characteristics of the luminophore were examined using DFT analysis. This sensor is highly efficient, with a high Stern-Volmer constant, making it ideal for on-site PA detection. It can be used to detect PA in real-time samples of matchstick head powders using test strips made from TLC plates through the drop-casting method.
The corresponding author (myself V. Vijayakumar) designed the work and the lab work has been completed by the first author (V. Hemalatha).

The authors declared that the work described has not been published previously that it is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. Authors in further declared that the submission of the manuscript is approved by all authors and tacitly or explicitly by the responsible authorities where the work was carried out, and that, if accepted, it will not be published elsewhere in the same form, in English or in any other language, including electronically without the written consent of the copyright-holder. Authors didn't use generative artificial intelligence (AI) and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process of the manuscript
V. Hemalatha: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. V. Vijayakumar: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:

V. Vijayakumar reports financial support was provided by Vellore Institute of Technology. V. Vijayakumar reports a relationship with Vellore Institute of Technology that includes: employment and funding grants. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: VV and VH gratefully acknowledge thanking the VIT management for characterization support and infrastructure. Authors thank full VIT management for seed grant (Grant number SG20230086)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.138322.

"Novel nanoporous Ti-phosphonate metal–organic framework for selective sensing of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol and a promising electrode in an energy storage device." ACS. Sustain. Chem. Eng. 9.42 (2021): 14224–14237 https://doi.org/10.1021/acssuschemeng.1c04877
